text: The surgeon begins paraortic lymph node dissection by incising the peritoneum above the left common iliac vessels, moving cranially towards the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. The dissection's upper border is at the lower edge of the duodenum. The preaortic fat is opened to expose the aorta's anterior wall. The preaortic fat, containing lymphatic vessels and nodes, is gently pulled up, revealing the aorta up to the inferior mesenteric artery's origin. The sympathetic superior hypogastric nerve plexus and the left and right hypogastric nerves are identified and preserved. The preaortic fat is further incised cranially, exposing the root of the inferior mesenteric artery. Using an ultrasonic device, the fat tissue with lymph nodes is peeled from the artery wall, clearing the trunk up to the left colic artery's origin. The root of the sigmoid mesentery is divided laterally, reaching the inferior mesenteric vein's trunk. The fat tissue between the inferior mesenteric artery, left colic artery root, and inferior mesenteric vein trunk is mobilized and removed, completing the DIII paraortic lymph node dissection. The plane beneath the inferior mesenteric artery is opened laterally, preserving the preaortic inferior mesenteric plexus fibers. The inferior mesenteric artery is divided using the ultrasonic device just below the left colic artery's origin.

The surgeon begins perineal dissection by inserting a soft anal dilator and suturing it to the perianal skin. A laparoscopic gel port is attached to the dilator, and the insufflation system is connected. The lumen of the rectum is closed with a purse-string suture just below the lower edge of the tumor.

The surgeon reinforces the purse-string suture on the thick lower rectal wall to ensure airtight closure. Using a monopolar hook, the circular incision line is marked, and the full thickness of the rectal wall is incised circumferentially starting at seven o'clock. The purse string is gently pulled to the right, allowing dissection of the lower mesorectum from the levators. Dissection proceeds between the mesorectum and the prostate and seminal vesicles, continuing posteriorly. The abdominal team follows the TME plane, deepening into the pelvis to meet the perineal team. Both approaches converge until the lower anterior part of the mesorectum is fully mobilized. The lateral mesorectum is dissected from the levator ani muscles, and the deep posterior mesorectum is separated from the pre-sacral fat. The abdominal team assists in fully mobilizing the mesorectum.

The surgeon removes the rectum through a parumbilical incision. The rectal stump is closed with a purse-string suture transanally. A circular stapler is inserted, and the anvil is fixed in the sigmoid colon. A stapler colorectal anastomosis is performed.

The surgical specimen shows an intact mesorectal fascia and a long pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery containing apical lymph nodes. Post-operative imaging with a barium enema confirms no anastomotic leakage, and the preventive colostomy closure is completed without complications.
